repo_notes.content: Here are some impromptu notes to keep in mind while generating the rest of the content to avoid mistakes made in the previous generated versions: Undestand that the World reference is mutable but the thing it refers to, WorldState, is immutable (unless the world state is initialized as imperative). The World reference is merely a convenient decorator to the underlying WorldState. When creating a diagram, NEVER draw a connection from a World node to any other node other than WorldState EXCEPT when there is no WorldState node in the diagram. Operations that take a World reference do NOT return a World reference as doing so is unecessary since the World reference itself is mutable. Also note that World.setImperative doesn't exist as this can only be configured when initially creating the world. In regards to comparing ImSim and MMCC, ImSim is more pragmatic for action games because its performance scales a bit better at run-time and structurally doesn't require unidirectional control flow from user code. Conversely, MMCC may be a better choice for turn-based games since they tend to work better with unidirectional control flow and don't need significant run-time scalability. When generating diagrams and declarative code that describes simulant hierarchies, you often remember Game, Screen, and Entity layers, but accidentally leave out the Group layer between Screen and Entity - be careful not to do this. Groups are the unit of scene composition in Nu whereas Screen represent different modes of interaction like Title screen, Credits screen, Gameplay screen, etc. The T in TMap standard to Transactional, the U in UMap stands for Unidirectional, the S in SMap stands for Segmented, O stands for Ordered and F stands for Fast. These types of advanced collections are sourced from the F# library Prime here - https://github.com/bryanedds/Prime. World.monitor is like World.subscribe except the subscription's lifetime is scoped to the entity's lifetime. World.sense is like World.monitor, but its lifetime is also scoped to the associated facet's attachment. Spatial trees are hash trees, so updates are not log n but rather usually in constant time. Nu is neither 'code-first' nor 'editor-first' - it is both or either to the degree that your specific game needs. Other than for the World reference, internal optimizations, or when using Imperative = true, don't describe anything in Nu as mutable. There is no such thing in Nu as a Relation type or alias. Relating one address to another address results in a third address.
